documents.title: Developing Guidelines for Generative AI Use

ai_strategy.summary: Saint Louis University has detailed complete AI guidelines that apply across academic, research, and administrative use, including AI subtypes such as generative and agentic AI. The guidance is backed by teaching resources and an M.S. in Artificial Intelligence, but public evidence of enterprise AI platform rollout is limited.

key_provisions: Use AI in accordance with academic integrity expectations and instructor or unit guidance
Do not enter restricted, confidential, regulated, or sensitive data into unapproved AI tools
Review AI outputs for accuracy, hallucination, bias, copyright, and attribution issues
Disclose or cite AI use when required by course, research, publisher, or unit rules
Keep human judgment and accountability in consequential academic, research, and administrative decisions
ai_policy.summary: Saint Louis University's AI policy posture is best characterized as comprehensive: public sources show explicit expectations for responsible use, privacy, disclosure, academic integrity, and human review, while leaving some implementation decisions to units or instructors.

bodies.name: Office of the Provost AI guidelines
Reinert Center for Transformative Teaching and Learning
bodies.scope: Faculty support for developing generative AI course guidelines.

governance_structure.summary: Saint Louis University's public AI governance is organized through Office of the Provost AI guidelines, Reinert Center for Transformative Teaching and Learning.

initiatives.title: Complete AI Guidelines
initiatives.description: SLU's guidelines cover ethical use, transparency, disclosure, privacy, human oversight, non-discrimination, restricted/confidential data, malicious use, training, and opt-out from model training where available.
initiatives.significance: Saint Louis University's complete ai guidelines is a signal of how an R1 institution is adapting AI across policy, teaching, research, operations, or workforce needs.

investment.description: No specific dollar amount disclosed in consulted public sources.

initiatives.policy_implications: Supports responsible AI adoption through human oversight, privacy controls, disclosure, or course-level expectations.

initiatives.title: Academic AI use rules
initiatives.description: SLU states instructor permission is required unless a syllabus grants permission; unauthorized AI use is treated similarly to unauthorized assistance or plagiarism.
initiatives.significance: Saint Louis University's academic ai use rules is a signal of how an R1 institution is adapting AI across policy, teaching, research, operations, or workforce needs.

initiatives.description: SLU's catalog lists a graduate M.S. in Artificial Intelligence with AI principles and elective coursework for applying AI and machine learning to problems.

initiatives.description: The Reinert Center provides resources for developing course-level generative AI guidelines, helping faculty make intentional and overt decisions about AI use.

initiatives.title: AI data and privacy controls
initiatives.description: SLU guidelines prohibit restricted, confidential, and personal information in generative AI tools unless contract language and ITS security controls permit it, with Madrid DPO approval where applicable.
initiatives.significance: Saint Louis University's ai data and privacy controls is a signal of how an R1 institution is adapting AI across policy, teaching, research, operations, or workforce needs.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Bias and non-discrimination provisions

regulatory_context.summary: Saint Louis University operates in Missouri; public AI guidance should be read alongside applicable state, federal, and institutional privacy, security, accessibility, and academic-integrity requirements.

narrative_summary: Saint Louis University (St. Louis, MO) is an R1 institution whose public AI posture shows activity across governance, teaching, research, enterprise tooling, workforce readiness, and access. Saint Louis University has detailed complete AI guidelines that apply across academic, research, and administrative use, including AI subtypes such as generative and agentic AI. The guidance is backed by teaching resources and an M.S. in Artificial Intelligence, but public evidence of enterprise AI platform rollout is limited. The most concrete public initiatives captured here are Complete AI Guidelines; Academic AI use rules; M.S. in Artificial Intelligence; Generative AI teaching resource guide; AI data and privacy controls. Across the profile, the strongest evidence comes from official university AI hubs, policy pages, teaching resources, research-center pages, and program pages rather than secondary reporting. The institution_type is set to r1_research from the canonical Carnegie R1 list. Known gaps are intentionally explicit: leadership names, user counts, detailed budgets, and compute counts are only included when they surfaced in public sources. Overall, the profile supports at least five AI initiatives and evidences strategy/leadership, governance, teaching/curriculum, research infrastructure, student services or enterprise tooling, workforce/community, partnerships, and equity dimensions where public sources permit.
